sources.notes: Statement and constraints: for every label decide whether it is a strict majority on some non-trivial tree path; total n <= 5e5.
Official editorial checked for the length-2/3 local witness and the alternative per-label virtual-tree plus tree-Kadane method.
Cross-checked that equal labels at distance at most two are necessary and sufficient and that closed-neighborhood scans implement the test.
Proved the distance-at-most-two equivalence and randomized it against exhaustive path-majority enumeration on small trees.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu solution index; the concrete consulted article is listed in sources.

annotation.summary: For every value x, decide whether some simple tree path of at least two vertices has x as a strict majority of its labels.
annotation.constraints: The total number of vertices is at most 5e5 and labels lie in [1,n], ruling out per-value traversals or arbitrary path enumeration.
annotation.core_idea: If x is a strict majority on a non-trivial path, two x occurrences must be at distance at most two: otherwise consecutive x occurrences have at least two non-x vertices between them, so x cannot outnumber non-x vertices. Conversely, two x vertices at distance one give [x,x], and at distance two give [x,other,x], both strict majorities. Thus x is feasible exactly when two x-labeled vertices have tree distance <=2. For every center u, count labels in the closed neighborhood {u} union N(u); any duplicate gives such a pair. The total scanned size is sum(deg(u)+1)=3n-2, so touched-frequency arrays yield O(n).
annotation.complexity: O(n) time and O(n) memory for the primary solution.
tricks: replace an arbitrary majority path by a length-2 or length-3 witness
equate feasibility with equal labels at distance at most two

solution_variants.name: closed-neighborhood-duplicate-scan
solution_variants.summary: For each vertex count labels of itself and its neighbors; mark every duplicate label and clear only touched counters.

solution_variants.name: per-label-virtual-tree-max-path
solution_variants.summary: For each label build the virtual tree of its vertices and LCAs, give matching vertices +1 and skipped vertices negative weight, then run a maximum-path-sum tree DP.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) total time with LCA preprocessing.

tags.evidence: A value is feasible exactly when two vertices carrying it are at distance one or two.
A strict majority forces two consecutive majority occurrences to have at most one intervening vertex.
The linear scan uses tree adjacency lists and sum of degrees.
The alternative compresses the tree separately to each label's vertices and their LCAs.
The virtual-tree alternative runs a Kadane-style maximum path-sum DP under +1/-1 weights.
LCA is required to build each per-label virtual tree and encode compressed path lengths.
The primary solution is an exact local-witness equivalence rather than a greedy choice.
